For a diff hunk where both ‘-’ and ‘+’ lines exist, our matching method does not consider the ‘+’ lines of the diff hunk unless the ‘-’ lines in the diff hunk is a subset of the ‘+’ lines (‘+’ or ‘-’ preﬁx omitted), and this type of diff hunk is generally used to change the code position.
Changelog. A changelog is a log or record of all notable

changes (e.g., bug ﬁxes, security patching, new features, etc.) made to a project (Fig. 1(c) shows an example). Some open- source projects include changelogs as one of the top-level ﬁles in their distributions. Each changelog corresponds to one open- source version, and one changelog is composed of multiple git logs. Changelogs need to be written according to certain speciﬁcation and its creation is a time-consuming manual pro- cess [33] . It is worth noting that some versions of software do not have changelogs, either because they were released before the changelog writing speciﬁcation was formed, or because the vendors simply did not write one. Thus, while we regard the information recorded in the changelogs as ground truth in our research, we do not assume the record list of changelogs for a software project is complete.
Git Log. It is a log generated by the command “git log [ <

options > ] [ < revision range > ] [[-] < path > ...]” [34] , and it can be used to show project history [35] , [36] , [37] , [38] and search for speciﬁc changes. Fig. 1(d) shows a part of the git log list, which is a collection of git logs in chronological order. Here each line represents a git log title, and the details recorded in a git log include the commit-id, the commit time of patch, diff hunks, the author and committer of this log (see Fig. 1(e) ). A git log is a modiﬁcation record of the source code, which is identiﬁed by a unique commit-id. Note that the developer can make new git commits for reasons other than ﬁxing bugs reported by CVEs; therefore, while each CVE-ID corresponds to a commit-id, many commit-ids have nothing to do with CVEs. Because git logs are recorded by the system program, we regard the information recorded in the git logs as ground truth in our research.
III. R ESEARCH G OALS AND C HALLENGES

Affected Software conﬁgurations section in NVD reports a list (or a set) of software versions impacted by the vulnerable source code. Prior research [14] , [15] (as well as our investigation) has shown that such information may be inaccurate. We ﬁnd that in most cases, the NVD includes in this section all versions whose version numbers are below the “up to” version (the last vulnerable version reported, see Fig. 1(a) ). Therefore, for each CVE-ID, we choose the false positive rate and the false negative rate as the performance metrics to quantify the inaccuracy of this set. We use developer log as evidence to accurately determine which versions are truly affected by a given CVE-ID . From another perspective, for a CVE-ID, we determine the boundary of vulnerable versions in the version tree that contains all the versions.
Challenges. There are three main challenges in this research.
(C1) Lack of convincing evidences. To identify and justify

the exact CVE affected vulnerable program versions, the most challenging task is to provide convincing evidences to the output of the methods. The veriﬁcation by PoC(Proof of Concept) is the most reliable, but not scalable and practical in our task. There are different methods and source of information regarding to the CVE affected versions, but they usually give conﬂicting results. As software source codes are changing over time, vulnerabilities are introduced, transformed, patched, and repatched with a new patch. We observed that a CVE patch present in a software version is neither a sufﬁcient nor a necessary condition to prove that the version is ﬁxed respect to the CVE. In this article, we assume that patches are correct and trustworthy. Therefore, if a patch is present in the software version, we consider a software version is ﬁxed; but if a patch is not present, we cannot decide if a version is really affected by only depending on patch presence test methods. (C2) Large scale of source codes of different versions. Since the set affected by the vulnerability may cover all published versions, how to efﬁciently and accurately identify the vulnerability version set for a large scale of software versions is a challenge. The traditional method is to check each software version separately. However, regardless of static or dynamic detection methods, with the continuous source code version updates, the number of software versions becomes very large, so the efﬁciency problem. (C3) Continuous transformation of the source code. Due to the continuous update and release of open source software versions, a vulnerability code may have undergone many changes in some earlier versions, which leads to incomplete matching between source code and the patch ﬁle for newer versions. Traditional detection methods relying on patch presence test are difﬁcult to accurately check such code whether there are vulnerabilities in the partial matching codes.
Our Approaches to the Challenges. To overcome the chal-

lenge C1, we leverage the information contained in version tree and developer logs because they contain ﬁrsthand and reliable evidences for the state transition of the vulnerabilities, which is presented in Sections IV-A , IV-B , and IV-D . For C2, we construct a version tree and ﬁnd the boundary of the affected versionsinthistreecollectively,insteadofcheckingeachversion separately, which is presented in Section IV-A and IV-C . For C3, We classify the different types of partial matching and trace the developer logs to counter this challenge, which is described in Section IV-D .
Overview of Our Method. Fig. 3 gives an overview of our

method, which consists of three steps. The required input in- cludes the CVE-IDs for an open-source software project under investigation, the set of open-source code for all its versions, and the developer logs (including the set of changelogs and the set of git logs corresponding to the CVE-IDs). The ﬁrst step is to build the version tree and obtain a version number set over which the vulnerability has been patched according to the commit-ids recorded in the changelogs. Based on this version set, the second step involves three different processing directions: (R1) to determine the last vulnerable version number in the trunk of the version tree, (R2) to determine the vulnerable
